critic_raw:
The GNN is trained on 'interaction graphs' representing social media and educational platform interactions, but the graph construction methodology is entirely unspecified. The choice of what constitutes a node (user? content item? session?), what constitutes an edge (co-view? comment? reply?), and how edge weights are assigned determines the entire inductive structure of the GNN. Without this specification, the GNN's claimed advantage in 'modeling relationships inferred from interaction graphs' cannot be assessed — a poorly constructed graph can make GNN performance worse than logistic regression, and a well-constructed one can make even a 1-layer GNN trivially superior. The comparison outcome is determined by graph engineering choices, not model architecture.

The baseline logistic regression receives 'identical features transformed into vector format.' If those features include graph-derived statistics (node degree, clustering coefficient, neighbor engagement rates), then the baseline is already benefiting from graph structure and the comparison is between GNN propagation and hand-engineered graph features — a very different test than GNN vs. non-graph model. The design must specify whether the baseline features are purely non-graph (user demographics, content metadata) or include graph statistics.

The stratified random split on user interaction data from a social network violates the exchangeability assumption of i.i.d. splitting more severely than most cases: in a social graph, users are connected to each other, so train/test nodes share edges. A GNN aggregates messages from neighbors, and if some neighbors are in the training set and some are in the test set, the GNN observes training-set information during test inference through neighborhood aggregation — a form of transductive leakage that is specific to GNN evaluation and not addressed by any design element.

AUROC is selected because it is 'threshold-independent,' but the downstream use case (broad engagement improvement) suggests the model will be deployed with a specific threshold for intervention targeting. A well-ordered but poorly calibrated GNN score provides no operational guidance for threshold selection, and calibration metrics are not mentioned.

The dataset spans two years with no description of user churn, new user onboarding, or content refresh rates. GNN models trained on a static graph snapshot fail badly when the graph structure changes (new users, deleted content, evolving interaction patterns). The design does not address temporal graph evolution or test on a time-shifted holdout to assess degradation under graph drift.
all_issues_raised: Graph construction methodology (node definition, edge definition, edge weighting) is entirely unspecified — the GNN's structural advantage is entirely determined by graph engineering choices, and the experiment cannot be replicated or interpreted without this specification.

Transductive GNN leakage: in a stratified random split, training and test nodes share graph edges, so GNN message passing at inference time aggregates information from training-set neighbors, violating the independence assumption and inflating test performance.

The baseline's feature set is ambiguous — if it includes graph-derived statistics (degree, clustering coefficient), the comparison tests GNN propagation vs. hand-engineered graph features rather than graph model vs. non-graph model.
No calibration metrics are reported; AUROC measures rank ordering but not probability calibration, which is required for operational threshold selection in engagement targeting.

The GNN is trained on a static 2-year graph snapshot with no evaluation of performance under graph drift (new users, content refresh, evolving interaction patterns), making deployment robustness untested.

No calibration metrics are reported; AUROC measures rank ordering but not probability calibration, which is required for operational threshold selection in engagement targeting.
